title: A Full House For Christmas

jokes.text: 80 People. Wow! Yeah, they got this really long counter at Woolworth's.
jokes.setup: Character mentions impressive-sounding sit-down dinner for 80 people
jokes.punchline: Reveals it was just eating at Woolworth's lunch counter
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection - sets up expectation of fancy dinner party, deflates with mundane reality. Well-structured setup/punchline.

jokes.text: I'd go crazy if I had to spend Christmas alone. You are spending Christmas alone. I am?
jokes.setup: Character expresses horror at spending Christmas alone
jokes.punchline: Other character points out they ARE spending Christmas alone
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective irony - character lacks self-awareness about their own situation.

jokes.text: I got feelings. Really? My mother's that way. Cries every Christmas on account of my little brother Nicky never comes to see her. I'll give you the same advice I give my mom when she cries. I say, 'Ma, shut up.'
jokes.setup: Louie claims to have feelings and understand sadness
jokes.punchline: His 'tender' advice to his crying mother is 'shut up'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character moment - thinks he's being sensitive while being completely insensitive. Dark humor that's character-specific.

jokes.text: I always knew you could be tender.
jokes.setup: Louie just told his crying mother to 'shut up'
jokes.punchline: Deadpan response calling this 'tender'
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan sarcasm - one word that completely undercuts Louie's attempt at sympathy.

jokes.text: Ma stayed up all night crying. It was really hard on me. I'll bet. Yeah, I hated having to put her out in the hall.
jokes.setup: Louie says it was hard on him that his mother cried all night
jokes.punchline: Reveals he literally put his crying mother out in the hallway
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy misdirection - audience thinks he means emotionally hard, but he literally kicked her out. Perfectly captures Louie's selfishness.

jokes.text: Come here. I want to introduce you to some of my inferiors.
jokes.setup: Louie wants to introduce his brother to his employees
jokes.punchline: Calls them 'my inferiors' instead of 'my employees'
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie - his casual arrogance and complete lack of social awareness in one phrase.

jokes.text: He never shows her any affection or consideration. Like me, I'm always saying stuff like, 'How you doing, Ma?'
jokes.setup: Louie criticizes his brother for not showing affection
jokes.punchline: His example of affection is a basic greeting
jokes.explanation: Louie's complete lack of self-awareness - thinks saying 'how you doing' makes him incredibly affectionate.

jokes.text: She cooks something, and I don't like the way it tastes, I never spit it out. I always tell her, 'Take another crack at it.'
jokes.setup: Louie continues listing his 'considerate' behaviors
jokes.punchline: His idea of being nice is not spitting out food and telling her to try again
jokes.explanation: Another perfect Louie moment - he thinks basic courtesy is extraordinary kindness. The phrasing 'take another crack at it' is particularly harsh.

jokes.text: she freezes up on me, Oh. You know? And she needs to go someplace where it's warm so she can thaw out.
jokes.setup: Louie explaining his mother's condition in winter
jokes.punchline: Talks about her like a frozen car engine that needs to 'thaw out'
jokes.explanation: Louie's mechanical way of describing human problems - treating his mother like a car that won't start.

jokes.text: 'Nicky, I'd really love to see your apartment in Vegas before I die, because if I don't I'll put a curse on you from my grave.'
jokes.setup: Louie told his mother to drop 'subtle hints'
jokes.punchline: His idea of subtle is threatening a curse from beyond the grave
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation joke - the complete opposite of 'subtle hints.' The specificity of a posthumous curse is perfectly absurd and very quotable.

jokes.text: Come on, give him a buck, everybody.
jokes.setup: Children's choir performing in the garage
jokes.punchline: Treating garage like a street corner for busking
jokes.explanation: Mild situational comedy - the incongruity of taking up a collection in a taxi garage.

jokes.text: I might have a New Year's eve gig for you.
jokes.setup: Children's choir just performed
jokes.punchline: Louie treating children like professional musicians he can book
jokes.explanation: Louie always looking for an angle - even with children's Christmas caroling.

jokes.text: How come? I don't know... tradition?
jokes.setup: Why haven't they had a Christmas party before
jokes.punchline: Complete non-answer delivered as if it makes perfect sense
jokes.explanation: Simple deadpan humor - the circular logic of not having tradition because of tradition.

jokes.text: You do exchange Christmas gifts, don't you? We do now.
jokes.setup: Enthusiastic character assumes everyone exchanges gifts
jokes.punchline: Awkward realization they're now obligated to buy gifts
jokes.explanation: Relatable workplace comedy - the awkwardness of unexpected gift obligation sprung on everyone.

jokes.text: You only been gone ten minutes. It takes ten minutes to drive there and back. What'd you expect, me to spend all afternoon with her?
jokes.setup: Nick was supposed to visit his mother after six years
jokes.punchline: His defensive math reveals he spent zero time with her
jokes.explanation: Dark character comedy - Nick's selfishness revealed through his own defensive logic. The math makes his cruelty obvious.

jokes.text: Hey, crazy lady, give me a break here.
jokes.setup: Phone rings during tense family moment
jokes.punchline: Louie answers business phone by calling customer 'crazy lady'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - even in family crisis, he can't help being rude to customers. Shows his priorities.

jokes.text: No, Mrs. De Palma, I don't know why people have children.
jokes.setup: Alex forced to talk to Louie's distraught mother
jokes.punchline: His response to her existential crisis is brutally honest
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response to an impossible situation. Alex's honesty in the face of family drama is both funny and relatable.

jokes.text: Nicky was a difficult birth, huh? No, no, please. Mrs. De Palma, don't tell me all the medical details. I'm really not a medical man, please.
jokes.setup: Alex trying to have polite conversation
jokes.punchline: Mrs. De Palma immediately overshares medical details
jokes.explanation: Classic oversharing comedy - Alex's polite question immediately backfires with TMI.

jokes.text: Nicky used to pull the what off flies?
jokes.setup: Mrs. De Palma continues oversharing about Nick's childhood
jokes.punchline: Alex's horrified reaction to disturbing information
jokes.explanation: Continuation of oversharing theme - audience imagination fills in the disturbing details.

jokes.text: You either take Ma with you when you go, or you leave town an only child.
jokes.setup: Louie threatening his brother
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic threat that sounds like murder
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - the euphemistic threat sounds sinister but is really just about disowning. Great economy and character-specific phrasing.

jokes.text: You hear the way she screeches? Sometimes when she's going real good, dogs come from all over.
jokes.setup: Explaining why he has to keep his promise to his mother
jokes.punchline: Describes her crying as so loud it attracts neighborhood dogs
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy through description - the image of dogs gathering from blocks away is absurd and specific.

jokes.text: Separate tabs.
jokes.setup: Alex offers to have a beer with Louie
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately clarifies they're not paying together
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie character moment - even in his emotional crisis, he's still cheap. Two words that reveal everything.

jokes.text: about Ma, about me, about Nick, about Ma and me, about Nick and me, about Nick and Ma.
jokes.setup: Louie saying he's been thinking
jokes.punchline: Lists every possible family combination in exhausting detail
jokes.explanation: Louie's obsessive nature - has to list every permutation rather than just saying 'family relationships.'

jokes.text: Thank goodness you don't have a bigger family.
jokes.setup: Louie's exhausting enumeration of family relationships
jokes.punchline: Alex's dry response imagining how much longer the list could be
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man response - Alex perfectly captures audience's feeling about Louie's verbal excess.

jokes.text: Do you know that I played the elf in the Christmas pageant every year till I left for the Army? I never got to play the parts that I wanted to play... Angel, shepherd, room clerk...
jokes.setup: Louie listing prestigious Christmas pageant roles he never got
jokes.punchline: Includes 'room clerk' as if it's a major Biblical character
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - starts with legitimate roles then casually includes 'room clerk' as if it's equally important. Perfect Louie logic.

jokes.text: The guy who said, 'No room at the Inn.' Oh, that room clerk.
jokes.setup: Louie's confusion about the 'room clerk' role
jokes.punchline: Alex clarifies the Biblical innkeeper connection
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up that clarifies the joke - Alex explains Louie's mangled reference to the innkeeper.

jokes.text: I want him wiped out, Alex crushed, destroyed, humiliated... for Ma.
jokes.setup: Louie wants revenge on his brother
jokes.punchline: Escalates to extreme violence then adds tender motivation
jokes.explanation: Great escalation and character work - builds to violent revenge then deflates with sweet motivation. Classic Louie contradiction.

jokes.text: Ace. Now I lost enough.
jokes.setup: Character cuts cards with Nick for $20
jokes.punchline: Loses with an ace and immediately quits gambling
jokes.explanation: Simple gambling humor - the quick realization that luck isn't changing.

jokes.text: Hey, could I have my shoes back? Yeah, but you can only borrow them till I'm done here.
jokes.setup: Player has apparently lost his shoes in the poker game
jokes.punchline: Can only borrow back his own shoes temporarily
jokes.explanation: Good visual comedy - the absurdity of gambling away your shoes and having to ask permission to wear them.

jokes.text: You're bankrolling him against me, your own brother? Jeez, Louie, I always knew you resented me and all of that but I never knew you had it in you to do something like this. You're all right.
jokes.setup: Nick discovers Louie is betting against him, seems hurt
jokes.punchline: Ends by complimenting Louie's ruthlessness
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Nick's admiration for ruthlessness reveals his own character while misdirecting from hurt feelings.

jokes.text: Maybe you better tell them to start without me.
jokes.setup: John calling his wife about Christmas dinner timing
jokes.punchline: Realizes the poker game will take much longer than expected
jokes.explanation: Relatable situation comedy - getting caught up in something when you should be elsewhere.

jokes.text: I once played for 72 hours in a row. Hey, 72 hours. That's like my fight with Rocky Santocuore. Your fight with Santocuore lasted four minutes. I'm talking about my recovery.
jokes.setup: Character compares poker endurance to boxing match duration
jokes.punchline: Reveals the 'fight' was actually the recovery time
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection - sets up tough guy story but deflates it by revealing he got beaten badly.

jokes.text: I'm reasonably sure.
jokes.setup: Louie desperately asks Alex to promise he's sure about his poker hand
jokes.punchline: Alex can only manage 'reasonably sure' with thousands at stake
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan response - the complete opposite of confidence when everything is on the line. Economy of language.

jokes.text: You said it was your life savings, Louie. I lied... I do that sometimes.
jokes.setup: Alex discovers he's been playing with company money, not Louie's savings
jokes.punchline: Louie's casual admission that he lies regularly
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie - casual admission of dishonesty with no shame. The understatement 'sometimes' makes it funnier.

jokes.text: What do you give me for a $300 watch? $80. Great, I only paid $50 for it.
jokes.setup: Alex trying to pawn his watch to cover the bet
jokes.punchline: Happy that he's losing less than he paid for it
jokes.explanation: Good irony - finding the silver lining in getting ripped off because he originally got a good deal.

jokes.text: You want me to bet Ma? You win? You're off the hook. You lose? You take her with you.
jokes.setup: Poker game escalating to desperate measures
jokes.punchline: Louie literally betting his mother as stakes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy escalation - treating his mother like poker chips. Perfectly captures the absurd extremes people go to.

jokes.text: You're betting on our own mother. Right. $300, Two weeks.
jokes.setup: Nick's shock at betting their mother
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately negotiates her value like a commodity
jokes.explanation: Continuation of the mother-as-commodity joke - Louie treating moral objections as negotiating tactics.

jokes.text: 100 is for hugs and kisses every day she's there. $50, You take her out to dinner twice a week to someplace fancy, not no taco palace. And the last $50 you take her to one of those splashy floor shows they got out there.
jokes.setup: Louie itemizing the terms of the mother bet
jokes.punchline: Detailed contract for maternal affection with specific pricing
jokes.explanation: Masterful escalation - pricing out maternal care like a business contract. The specificity ('not no taco palace') makes it even funnier. Peak Louie.

jokes.text: not no taco palace
jokes.setup: Louie specifying restaurant requirements for his mother
jokes.punchline: His idea of 'someplace fancy' is defined by what it's NOT
jokes.explanation: Good character detail showing Louie's limited worldview. The specific exclusion is funnier than a positive requirement.

jokes.text: It's $4,350, three weeks in Vegas six meals, one floor show, one plane ticket, one watch and a pair of shoes. Oh, yeah, and the hugs and kisses.
jokes.setup: Someone keeping track of the escalating bizarre bet
jokes.punchline: Recaps the completely surreal itemized wager
jokes.explanation: Great callback that recaps the absurdity - the casual addition of 'hugs and kisses' to the material goods list is perfect.

jokes.text: Three sixes, you lose. I lose? You lose! I'll have Ma packed and waiting for you.
jokes.setup: The climactic poker hand reveal
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately shifts to practical logistics
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff - Louie's instant shift to practical matters shows he planned this outcome. Great character consistency.

jokes.text: ♪ Hallelujah! ♪
jokes.setup: Nick has just lost and must take his mother to Vegas
jokes.punchline: Celebratory singing as if a miracle has occurred
jokes.explanation: Good emotional release - the crew treating this manipulation like divine intervention provides comic relief.

tmdb_overview: After John smashes up Cab 804, the cabbies reminisce about their experiences in the car.
insight: Christmas episode sustains 82 score through character beats, not punchlines—a rarity for Taxi's formula.
